result:
I. The Warehouse at 3:17 a.m.  
The forklift’s orange warning light sweeps across the rafters like a low-budget lighthouse. I am not supposed to be here—no one is—but the security guard is trading memes with a long-distance girlfriend in Manila, and the cameras loop yesterday’s footage because the shift supervisor wanted to practice his golf swing in receiving bay C. The building exhales dust, cardboard, and the faint sweetness of rat bait. I wander the aisles, pretending I am in the Library of Alexandria after hours, every pallet a scroll, every barcode a hieroglyph. I scan a box of off-brand vacuum filters and feel the irrational urge to adopt it, give it a name, teach it to read. Somewhere a shrink-wrap machine sighs like an old man settling into a bath. I think: if I speak softly enough, the building might confess its real name. We are both insomniacs, after all.

II. A Brief History of My Accent  
I was born on the fault line between two dialects. My mother spoke the flattened vowels of the Great Lakes, where “bag” rhymes with “vague” if you’re careless. My father carried the maritime lilt of Nova Scotia, a place that still believes in the future perfect tense. The first word I mispronounced was “mirror,” which I rendered as “mere,” causing my kindergarten teacher to suspect cataracts. For years I believed that one’s reflection was a mere, a pond you could step into if you tried hard enough. When I finally saw the Atlantic, it sounded like every sentence I’d ever finished with an upward curl. I dipped my fingers in the tide and tasted my own consonants.

III. The Secret Life of Secondary Characters  
In every novel there is a waitress who refills coffee without being asked, a neighbor who borrows jumper cables, a receptionist who announces the protagonist is late for therapy. They have no surnames, no childhood traumas beyond the generic, yet they persist. I like to imagine them clocking out, removing their badges, boarding the same night bus. They sit three rows apart, never speaking, each convinced the others are protagonists. The waitress practices German with an app because one day she’ll surprise the tourist couple who undertip. The receptionist writes haiku on Post-its and hides them inside MRI machine manuals. The neighbor keeps a ledger of every time he’s been useful, sure that when the page is full he’ll level up into a love interest. Their stories intersect only in the index of the author’s subconscious, somewhere between “coffee, 37, 88, 209” and “jumper cables, 142.”

IV. Why I Still Write Postcards  
Email is a river; postcards are fossils. You can hold a postcard up to the light and see the pressure mark where the pen paused, the microscopic crater from a period. I write them in laundromats, on ferries, in hospital waiting rooms where vending machines sell hope in foil bags. I always begin with the weather, not because it matters but because it is the gatekeeper of small talk, the bouncer who decides whether the rest of the sentence may enter. “Dear S—, The fog here sublets the horizon.” I never mention monuments; monuments can Google themselves. Instead I report the toddler who tried to feed Cheetos to a sculpture, the elderly couple arguing about whether seagulls have accents. I address them to people I no longer speak to, convinced the postcard will arrive the exact day they finally forgive me. The postage goes up a cent every few years, a quiet inflation of regret.

V. The Ontology of Unused Stationery  
There is a drawer in my desk devoted to paper too beautiful to desecrate. Cream laid stock with a watermark of apple blossoms. Vermillion sheets that feel like petting a horse. A single envelope the color of 4 a.m. snowfall, so delicate I once tried to inhale it. They are the domestic equivalent of vintage cars: admired, insured, never driven. I tell myself I am saving them for a moment equal to their pulp, a sentence so necessary it deserves pearlescent permanence. Meanwhile, grocery lists proliferate on the backs of receipts, and apologies go drafted in the notes app, autocorrected into cowardice. The beautiful pages age like unmarried aunts, developing a faint brittleness around the edges. Sometimes, drunk on insomnia, I take one out and let it warm under my palm, the way a midwife warms a newborn. I whisper, “Not yet, not yet,” and slide it back into exile. One day I will write my own name on the final sheet, seal it, mail it to myself return-to-sender, and achieve the closed loop of narcissism.

VI. A Field Guide to the Forgotten Smells of Childhood  
1. The inside of a plastic Halloween mask after you’ve breathed into it for three consecutive hours of Trick-or-Treat, a bouquet of condensation and burnt sugar.  
2. The rubbery tang of inflatable arm floaties, suggestive of both safety and drowning.  
3. The metallic sigh of a hot-water radiator waking up in September, smelling like pennies swallowed by a ghost.  
4. The adhesive strip on library cards, a marriage of vinegar and anticipation.  
5. The underside of a sticker freshly peeled from its backing, equivalent to licking a battery made of fruit.  
6. The inside of your parents’ car the morning after a long drive, when upholstery and dreams still hang suspended in the upholstery.  
I keep these in a mental mason jar, unscrewing the lid whenever the present smells too much like adulthood: printer toner, refrigerator paranoia, the ozone of unanswered texts. One whiff and I am eight again, convinced that time is a staircase you can run up two steps at a time if your legs are short enough.

VII. The Paradox of the Midnight Bakery  
The city mandates that every block contain at least one bakery operating between 11 p.m. and 5 a.m. No one remembers passing the ordinance, but the neon signs obey. Inside, fluorescent lights hum like anxious parents. The baker—always one, never two—wears a apron dyed indigo by years of blueberry stains. You may order anything, provided you can name its opposite. A customer asks for “light,” receives a pitch-black loaf that tastes like the inside of a cave. Another requests “solitude,” gets a croissant shaped like two interlocking hands. No currency changes palms; payment is a memory you can no longer access. I once asked for “the future.” The baker handed me a still-warm baguette that screamed faintly. I ate it on the curb, cried butter, and have never again feared tomorrow.

VIII. An Open Letter to My Former Neighbor’s Wind Chimes  
Dear Tubular Menaces,  
I lied when I told the landlord you sounded “ethereal.” You sounded like someone teaching skeletons to waltz. At 2 a.m. you were the soundtrack to every mistake I’d ever archived, a xylophone of regret. I fantasized about climbing the fire escape with garden shears, but feared the clatter of your murder would only multiply. Instead I wrote you a sonnet in iambic pentameter, each line ending with the word “please.” I folded it into a paper airplane and threw it into the storm you summoned. The next morning you were gone, replaced by a silence so absolute it rang. I still don’t know whether you broke or achieved enlightenment. Either way, I forgive us both.

IX. The Economy of Lost Buttons  
Every missing button enters an underground barter system. The shirt donates its spare to the jacket, which later bequeaths a snap to the overstuffed cushion. In this economy, nothing is discarded, only demoted. I once found a jar of buttons at an estate sale labeled “Extras from a Life Lived Fully.” Inside: mother-of-pearl from a wedding blouse, brass from a cadet uniform, a single red plastic disk stamped “Burger Chef 1979.” I bought the jar for three dollars, which is the going rate for nostalgia minus inflation. At home I poured them onto the carpet like pirate loot. They hummed, recognizing one another across decades. I’ve started sewing them onto the inside hem of my curtains, a private constellation. When the sun hits, the room fills with tiny eclipses, each one a former gateway to skin.

X. A Recipe for Recreating Tuesday  
Ingredients:  
1. One unbruised Tuesday, preferably from mid-October.  
2. A bus transfer left in a coat pocket, dated the year you still believed in omens.  
3. Thirty milliliters of the hum that fluorescent lights make when they pretend to be stars.  
4. The exact weight of your father’s silence during a commercial break.  
5. A spoonful of the moment you realized you were taller than your mother.  
6. Coffee cooled to the temperature of apologies that arrive too late.  
Method:  
Preheat nostalgia to 325°F. Grease a nine-inch memory with butter you forgot to refrigerate. Fold ingredients together using the same wooden spoon you once used as a microphone. Do not overmix; lumps are where the song gets stuck. Pour into the pan, slide onto the middle rack of your earliest kitchen. Bake until the edges brown like the photographs you promised to organize. Let cool on a windowsill where neighborhood dogs can smell time rising. Serve inverted onto a plate you stole from college. Serves one, but leftovers keep indefinitely in the freezer labeled “do not defrost.”

XI. The Cartography of Inappropriate Laughter  
There is a map taped inside my skull charting every place I’ve laughed when I absolutely should not have:  
1. During a eulogy, when the priest mispronounced “celibate” as “celebrate.”  
2. In the elevator of a Children’s Hospital, when the recorded voice said “going down” with accidental nihilism.  
3. At my own deposition, opposing counsel’s necktie featured dancing tacos.  
Each laugh began as a hiccup of pure existential fright, then blossomed into a sound I disguised as a cough. The map is drawn in arterial ink; every X pulses when memory presses it. Legend includes: skull-and-crossbones for near-expulsion, dotted line for the escape route via fire-stairwell shame. Sometimes, late at night, I unfold it like a perverse treasure chart, wondering what uncharted island of doom will next host my hysteria. The ocean between the islands is composed of everyone who has ever glared at me, their eyes floating like unmoored lighthouse bulbs.

XII. Why I Abandoned the Novel About the Woman Who Could Only Speak in Questions  
At first she was charming: “Would you like sugar in your crisis?” “Is the moon a rehearsal?” But every scene demanded answers she couldn’t provide. Dialogue became a tennis match where the ball multiplied mid-air. The love interest tried to propose; she replied, “Does forever come with a return policy?” He fled to a neighboring novella. I attempted compromise—allowed her one declarative per chapter—but it emerged as an apology: “I’m sorry.” The other characters formed a union, demanded statements, clauses, certainty. Plot lines tangled into an interrogative knot that even I, omnipotent in theory, could not untie. One night I deleted the file. My laptop asked, “Are you sure?” I answered yes, thereby breaking her only rule.

XIII. The Museum of Unfinished Gestures  
Imagine a gallery where every handshake aborted mid-reach is preserved under glass. The near-hug that became an awkward pat. The wave mistaken for stretching. They float in formaldehyde mist, accompanied by placards: “Offered to ex-best friend, 2014, reason: politics.” Visitors wear headphones playing the sound of almost: a sigh that could’ve been hello, the creak of a chair leaning closer then thinking better. In the gift shop you can buy replicas of your own aborted embrace, freeze it on a bookshelf as a reminder of what muscles memorized and forgot. I volunteer there every third Sunday, guiding tourists through the Hall of Near Misses. We end at a mirror that reflects you one second delayed; people wave at themselves, forever closing the loop they left open in eighth grade.

XIV. The Year My Plants Went on Strike  
It began with the pothos submitting a list of demands: better humidity, fewer Radiohead songs, an end to being used as a coat rack. The snake plant unionized with the succulents, citing ideological differences about water. Negotiations stalled when I refused to recognize photosynthesis as labor. They staged a slow withering, dropping leaves like pamphlets: “Your Emotional Neglect Has Chlorophyll Consequences.” I hired a mediator—a retired botanist who spoke fluent wilting. Settlement included weekly misting, rotated exposure to Joni Mitchell, and a nondisclosure about the quantity of leftover pasta water. The fern acted as though it had won, but I caught it practicing triumphant poses at 3 a.m. Growth resumed, yet something lingered, a certain stiffness in new stems, as if every leaf signed its employment contract in visible ink.

XV. How to Fold a Fitted Sheet with Dignity  
Start by acknowledging defeat. Hold the sheet like a defeated sail, corners limp as yesterday’s enthusiasm. Whisper, “You are not my enemy, merely my geometry teacher.” Locate the corner seams, those secret pockets where dreams go to lint. Tuck one into the other until you form a lopsided heart—this is the closest you will come to confessions in adulthood. Lay it on a flat surface, ideally the bed of a former lover who claimed you were “too much.” Smooth the wrinkles with the heel of your hand, counting each crease as a day you did not text them. Fold twice, thrice, until the rectangle resembles a parcel of regret small enough to store. Place in closet. Promise to launder memories next week. Break promise. Repeat monthly.

XVI. The Last Payphone in the City  
It stands outside a shuttered dive bar, receiver dangling like an afterthought. Someone scrawled “call for forgiveness” above the coin slot. I insert a quarter; the dial tone sounds like my mother humming while chopping onions. I punch the number of my childhood landline, now assigned to a sushi restaurant. A man answers, “Dragon Roll Supreme.” I ask if they serve redemption. He says, “Only with spicy mayo.” I hang up, leave the quarter as a tip for the past. A pigeon lands on the cord, adopts it as a perch. In its eye I see reflected every message I never left: I’m sorry, I’m here, I’m almost home. The bird coosonce, a busy signal, and takes flight, carrying my unanswered calls into the circuitry of sky.

XVII. Dream in Which I Am the Inventory at a Failed Estate Sale  
I wake up priced: right elbow—$3, slight chip; childhood recollection of snow—$15, yellowed; ability to wink—$2, intermittent. Strangers pick me up, test for cracks, haggle over the trauma section. A woman in a vintage coat lifts my fear of elevators, sniffs it like melon, sets it back down. The auctioneer is my eleventh-grade English teacher, gavel shaped like a red pen. “Going once, going twice—” No taker for the lot. I am marked for donation to an after-school program where kids will glue macaroni to my regrets. I try to shout, “I’m still using that!” but my voice is boxed with the VHS tapes. At the last minute, a child chooses me because I am free. She carries me out in a paper bag that reads, “New Owner.” In her bedroom she unpacks me like Christmas, lays each piece on the carpet, names them star, moon, lighthouse. She keeps the fear of elevators, uses it as a step-stool to reach the cookie jar. Value, it turns out, is just another word for repurposed.

XVIII. The Quiet After the Car Stops but Before You Open the Door  
Engine ticks itself to sleep. Key dangles like a metronome counting down to reentry. Inside this capsule you are momentarily stateless: neither where you were nor where you must next pretend to be. The radio has ended but the silence retains its shape, a mold you could pour future selves into. Streetlights stripe the windshield like barcodes for unsold hours. You rehearse expressions: the smile for the cat, the apology for the overdraft, the neutral face you wear to check the mail. Each one fits poorly, thrift-store costume. You wonder what would happen if you simply stayed seated, fossilized in bucket-seat amber. The neighborhood holds its breath, waiting for the domestic avalanche your arrival will trigger. You inhale, feel the seat belt sigh off your collarbone, and open the door, releasing you back into the narrative you pay rent to maintain.

XIX. Why I Keep a Failure Résumé  
Page one: jobs I did not get (arctic tour guide, subtitle writer for silent films, professional apologizer). Page two: relationships that expired like milk—some chunky, some merely sour. Page three: manuscripts composted, inventions that would have revolutionized nothing (left-handed spoon, edible usb). I print it on red paper so it cannot be mistaken for success’s anemic cousin. Once a year I update fonts, add new rejections like merit badges. I read it aloud to an empty couch, applaud politely. The document serves as anti-motivation: see how little the world has missed. Paradoxically, it comforts. The gap between attempt and achievement is where the air tastes of possibility. Without failure, I would be all conclusion, a book whose final page is glued to the cover. Instead I remain mid-sentence, a comma curling toward the next clause, confident that tomorrow I can add another line in red.

XX. Postscript Written at Dawn on the Back of the Failure Résumé  
The sun infiltrates the blinds, strips the room like interrogation. The red page now glows pink, the color of scraped knees the day you learned bikes obey gravity. I flip it over, write: “Today I will try to be the person my dog believes I am when I open the cookie jar.” Beneath that: “If I fail, see above résumé, add entry: ‘Attempted humanity, results pending.’” The ink bleeds, merges with old rejections, forming a Rorschach that looks suspiciously like an open door. I fold the page into a paper airplane, aim it at the wastebasket, miss. It lands beneath the window, wings twitching in the updraft of radiator clank. I let it stay there, a tiny red bird learning to fly upward into the heating duct, carrying its cargo of almosts toward some attic of eventual forgiveness.

I am marked for donation to an after-school program where kids will glue macaroni to my regrets. I try to shout, “I’m still using that!” but my voice is boxed with the VHS tapes. At the last minute, a child chooses me because I am free. She carries me out in a paper bag that reads, “New Owner.” In her bedroom she unpacks me like Christmas, lays each piece on the carpet, names them star, moon, lighthouse. She keeps the fear of elevators, uses it as a step-stool to reach the cookie jar. Value, it turns out, is just another word for repurposed.

XVIII. The Quiet After the Car Stops but Before You Open the Door  
Engine ticks itself to sleep. Key dangles like a metronome counting down to reentry. Inside this capsule you are momentarily stateless: neither where you were nor where you must next pretend to be. The radio has ended but the silence retains its shape, a mold you could pour future selves into. Streetlights stripe the windshield like barcodes for unsold hours. You rehearse expressions: the smile for the cat, the apology for the overdraft, the neutral face you wear to check the mail. Each one fits poorly, thrift-store costume. You wonder what would happen if you simply stayed seated, fossilized in bucket-seat amber. The neighborhood holds its breath, waiting for the domestic avalanche your arrival will trigger. You inhale, feel the seat belt sigh off your collarbone, and open the door, releasing you back into the narrative you pay rent to maintain.

XIX. Why I Keep a Failure Résumé  
Page one: jobs I did not get (arctic tour guide, subtitle writer for silent films, professional apologizer). Page two: relationships that expired like milk—some chunky, some merely sour. Page three: manuscripts composted, inventions that would have revolutionized nothing (left-handed spoon, edible usb). I print it on red paper so it cannot be mistaken for success’s anemic cousin. Once a year I update fonts, add new rejections like merit badges. I read it aloud to an empty couch, applaud politely. The document serves as anti-motivation: see how little the world has missed. Paradoxically, it comforts. The gap between attempt and achievement is where the air tastes of possibility. Without failure, I would be all conclusion, a book whose final page is glued to the cover. Instead I remain mid-sentence, a comma curling toward the next clause, confident that tomorrow I can add another line in red.

XX. Postscript Written at Dawn on the Back of the Failure Résumé  
The sun infiltrates the blinds, strips the room like interrogation. The red page now glows pink, the color of scraped knees the day you learned bikes obey gravity. I flip it over, write: “Today I will try to be the person my dog believes I am when I open the cookie jar.” Beneath that: “If I fail, see above résumé, add entry: ‘Attempted humanity, results pending.’” The ink bleeds, merges with old rejections, forming a Rorschach that looks suspiciously like an open door. I fold the page into a paper airplane, aim it at the wastebasket, miss. It lands beneath the window, wings twitching in the updraft of radiator clank. I let it stay there, a tiny red bird learning to fly upward into the heating duct, carrying its cargo of almosts toward some attic of eventual forgiveness.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
